title: Chapter 7 - Ports, TCP, and UDP
description: Quick concept-check about ports and transport protocols.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a network port number?

options.label: To identify a specific service or application on a device
To name the device on the internet
To identify the physical network cable

explanations.overview: Ports route traffic on a device to the correct service.

byOptionId.a: Correct — ports let multiple services share one IP address.
byOptionId.d: Physical cables are not ports in this sense.
byOptionId.c: The IP address names the device, not the port.
byOptionId.b: Ports work together with IP addresses, not replace them.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes TCP compared to UDP?

options.label: TCP guarantees ordered delivery and retries lost packets
TCP is connectionless and faster than UDP
TCP is used only for video streaming

explanations.overview: TCP focuses on reliable, ordered delivery; UDP focuses on low latency.

byOptionId.b: Correct — TCP ensures reliable, ordered delivery.
byOptionId.d: TCP is used for many services, not only streaming.
byOptionId.a: TCP maintains order; UDP may not.

questions.prompt: Which port is commonly used for HTTPS?

byOptionId.d: Correct — 443 is the standard HTTPS port.

questions.prompt: Which transport protocol generally guarantees ordered, reliable delivery?

explanations.overview: TCP provides connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery; UDP does not.

byOptionId.c: UDP is connectionless and does not guarantee order.
byOptionId.b: Correct — TCP ensures ordered, reliable delivery.
byOptionId.d: IP is used for addressing/routing, not delivery guarantees at this layer.
byOptionId.a: HTTP is an application protocol on top of TCP (usually).
